title: Gloria Willis -Operations Interview | Events Marketing Manager
meeting_title: Gloria Willis -Operations Interview | Events Marketing Manager

transcript.text: Thank you.
Thank you.
Thank Thank you.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Hi, Gloria.
Hi there.
How are you?
Good.
Nice to meet you.
Nice to meet you as well.
Happy Friday.
Yes, happy Friday.
And hopefully you can't hear too much background noise.
This is the day that my apartment decided to have people working in the trees right outside of my apartment.
Well, let me know.
I can't hear it, but let me know if you can't hear me or something.
Perfect, perfect.
Nice.
Well, I was watching your interview with Tucker already, so we can probably skip some of the resume stuff.
But I did want to start.
Okay, first, let me introduce myself.
So I'm Bridget.
I run the ops teams here.
And then my background is a bit more in go-to-market, so I always, like, gravitate towards helping with go-to-market stuff.
So we're closely with – we have a very small go-to-market team.
We have one AE, who just started, and then Marcel, who does 80% of everything.
And then we have Jason, who's our head of growth.
And that's pretty much it.
Like, we have –
We used some contractors on the side here and there, but pretty lean team for sure.
So yeah, so we're very excited about this role.
Marcel's marketing vision in general is very events heavy and very community focused.
He's huge on like his network is like his everything and it's brought all the business we have so far.
So he's very like, it's very important to him to like have good connecting experiences and to me and try to build up as big of a orbit as we can across like GTM people.
So yes, yeah, I looked at his LinkedIn last night and noticed we have, I think, like 12 connections, one of which is Sam Jacobs, which was the CEO of Pavilion, which was my last role.
So I did there, so I was like, oh, they're connected.
Interesting.
I would not be surprised if they're like friends or something that Marcel knows everyone.
It's so crazy, but he puts in
My gosh, that guy is, like, networking nonstop.
Oh, yeah.
Cool.
Well, so I have a few questions we can go through in a bit.
But, like, I actually was, like, very curious about, like, hold on.
I was just pulling back up your resume.
So, yeah, tell me about the Pavilion stuff.
Like, super interested to hear about that.
Yes.
So Pavilion is actually the reason why I'm looking for another role right now.
So let me tell you a little bit about the role.
So I was hired on as their, I was actually recruited by them to, as their director of events.
And they kind of had an events team, but they were mostly, like, customer service people kind of just, like, doing events on the side.
So I was their first event hire.
So it was my job to kind of come in and build out what the event platform, what event experience would feel like and experience would be.
So I came in.
had a team of three.
So
And we managed all of their salon dinners, which was global, global events.
did them all over the world.
We also had their, we had the CMO Summit, CRO Summit, and the CEO Summit.
And then the year that I left, I had just finished doing the go-to-market.
Go-to-market 2023 is the one that I did.
And that was an event for 700 people or so, several sponsors, several speakers, obviously 700 attendees, three different tracks.
Not really tracks, but we have three different lines that people can go through with their choice of sessions.
So I managed, I managed everything.
I built it, built it from the ground.
And then a lot of what they're doing today is because of the work that, the work that I helped build.
Amazing.
What were the, and these dinners, what was that for?
Or like, what was this?
Mm-hmm.
So it was, it was actually, it served.
So purposes.
So one, obviously, Pavilion is a community.
So we want to make sure we're creating environments where the executive level people can actually have dinner with someone, learn from each other.
Sometimes we actually have a speaker come in.
And then the other portion of that was actually it was helping drive revenue.
So we would have sponsors for those.
So we would get sponsors.
They would pay for a portion.
They would pay for a certain amount of dinners per year.
And then we would cover the dinners.
And we would plan them in-house.
And then the sponsor would get like five to seven minute max to be able to kind of introduce themselves, tell them about what they do.
And then the rest of the program was completely about a roundtable.
So we always had a moderator host.
So whatever host city was, if it was in San Francisco, the chair for that, for San Francisco, would go to the dinner and would help moderate.
And rest of And they would have- And

They would go ready with, like, maybe three to five questions, and they would go around each table, depending on how many people were there, and really just present the questions and have real, real down-to-earth, raw, quick conversations.
And was it, like, was it for a specific audience or all the audiences?
Because you mentioned, like, some of the summits were for CROs versus CMOs versus CEOs.
Yes.
So originally, they were just for any executive that was a part of being a member.
And then they have now moved into being, like, whatever the sponsor is.
If the sponsor is more on AI, then they'll specifically target a different team.
Yeah.
Or, like, a finance SaaS company or something for CFOs.
I see.
Yes.
Cool.
Okay.
So I see.
Because I'm looking at the pavilion site now.
So the private salon dinners is included in the, like, executive membership.
Yes.
So how many?
I'd love to go.
How many a year are there?
It had to, it was a lot.
I mean, if you're scrolling through.
civilian members are there?
Well, when I left, we were just shy of 10,000.

So yeah, a lot, a lot of members, but that, that ranged from the entry level all the way up to the executive level members, as well as one more tier above that, which was their CEO membership, which was a little, little different from the offerings for the rest of them.
Okay.
Then one of my questions, which maybe it could be related to this or something else, doesn't have to be Pavilion, but what's like a very unique event that you've designed?
Unique event.
I mean, they've all been really fun.
I did one at, I don't know if it was unique, but it was, it was very fun.
It was, I did an event with Opus Bank.
Unique event.
partnered with a former RMIT.
We're Thank
Basketball player.
And he actually came in and did a fireside chat with our CEO.
And we gave the executives who went to that, to that event, the opportunity to kind of engage with this former NBA player.

And it was like, it was just a really special experience because you don't always get access to those kind of, those kind of people, especially ones that are like out of the game because you don't get to see them play anymore.
So that was, that was fun.
Was that for prospects or clients or investors or what?
It was predominantly for prospects.
So when I was at Opus, we did, most of our events were for B2B.
So we were a bank that didn't really focus on like the consumer side.
We were a bank for businesses.
So we, I did a lot of events that were just for like local businesses looking to find a new bank.
Got it.
Was that expensive getting the NBA player?
Well, yes.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
It is expensive.
But, you know, the unique events usually are.
Bespoke experiences are, they're priced, but they're worth it.
I mean, I did one where we worked with this company called Resident in New York, where they rent out private houses, and then they bring in a chef, and then they moderate the conversation.
So you get access to this, like, penthouse in New York, and you have this sit-down dinner with bartenders serving, and it was for, like, 14 people.
So it was very intimate, very cool.
I did a CEO, the CEO Summit that I did, their winter program.
They went to Ireland.
So I helped that entire trip, and that trip included helicopter rides.
And, yeah, so, like, it was, I've done some interesting and fun experiences.
Marcina loves all these things.
Like, not because he's, like, super fancy or anything.
He's just, like, he's, like, I'm, he's a known CMO, and so he gets invited.
It's everything.
And he's like, at this point, like, he's like, I have a two-year-old, like, I need to go home.
I need to, like, I got to go to the, I want to go to the cool stuff these days.
Yep, yep.
I was, it's funny you say that.
I was actually just putting together Drone Deploy.
We're looking to do more executive activations in 2026, if I'm here.
Mm-hmm.
So they have tasked me with kind of thinking through what it is that actually executives want to experience at their events.
And I just took the time to just boil it down to one sentence.
And that one sentence is, is like, they want to do things that's worth their time.
Yeah.
We cannot have them going to something that they can do on their own.
And if it's something that they can do on their own, those little details are going to be what makes it a special standout.
So it could be, they're always going to get wine at something, but are they going to get a wine that has their logo on it, etched in it from the year that they opened up?
Like, no, probably not.
But that's the kind of things that you want to be doing for these.
Really cool bespoke events.
I know.
Yeah, it's funny because I also run finance here, so it's taken me months to get used to the ideas.
I'm always like, oh my God, Marcel, I'm going to have a heart attack at that idea.
And he's like, got to trust me.
What about, okay, what else was I going to ask?
Oh yeah, what about, have you, well, first of all, Drone Deployer, like how big is that company?
How many people work there?
We're at, I believe, a little over 300 people now.
Okay, and are there other events people or is it just you?
I have one direct report to me.
And what do they do?
She focuses on our trade shows.
So I manage our high touch proprietary programs and she handles all of our industry events.
Got it.
We haven't really done industry events.
So we're going to
Like in two weeks, we're going to do a happy hour during an industry event, but not like to do with it.
Like we rented a roof bar in SF, I think it's called 25 Lusk or something.
That'll be during inbound is what the conference is called.
But yeah, I don't know.
I don't know if Marcel, what he thinks about trade shows, actually.

I feel like ours is kind of different because like, because you're like a platform, like a set more of the SaaS play where we're like kind of like, like an agency at the moment, like an agency play with very like high touch, like high, like you got to get into the CMO.
And they're not usually the ones walking around the trade show floor.
Well, they are when it's go to market 2025.
So that is one.
The Lens event is just for executives.
They actually, they, we looked at every single registration that came in.
And if they didn't have the right title, they were automatically declined.
So you do have CMOs going to that event.
Yeah, that's true.
So yeah.
Yeah.
Their sponsorships are, I mean, when I was there, they were a little bit more reasonably priced.
I don't know what they are now because I haven't looked.
But I can see that being, if you're going to do one, I would say that that could be an interesting one to do.
Where is it?
This year it's in Washington, D.C.
I don't know where they're going to have it next year.
Yeah, that's cool.
I mean, it would be also amazing to get Marcel speaking.
He's such a good speaker.
Maybe they usually get you to pay for a sponsorship to get a speaking slot.
No, they actually source their speakers.
So the breakouts, yes, you do have to pay to get a spot there.
But they do source all of their speakers.
And I'm still very close with the CMO of there, Kathleen.
So I could absolutely reach out and see him.
Absolutely.
And on the partnership side, was very close with Aaron.
He's the one who manages the partnerships for sponsorships.
Very close.
Just reach out.
Is your current company going to that?
Or is it not really the right audience?
It's not the right audience.
So right now I went from full-blown all things go-to-market to construction workers, oil and gas, and drones, which is why I'm looking for my role.
Because I miss the go-to-market people.
Like, I want to go back to that world.
So, I mean, everything's fine with my current role.
So everything's fine, except that I miss building something, and I miss my go-to-market people.
Yeah.
Tell me, like, okay, if we were doing a dinner, like an executive dinner, a happy hour, something like, what are you normally doing when those events are happening?
Like, you're there, obviously, but, like, what's your – I don't know.
How do you like to be at the events?
I am the watcher.
So I am – I'm making sure that people are comfortable.
I am watching to see if somebody's kind of, like, noticing that the table's wiggly or whatnot, and then I'm going immediately to the restaurant.
Fathom being like, you guys need to fix this.

I'm the one where if I overhear someone saying, this actually just happened at one of my last events in Chicago, he was talking about this amazing steak that he had from that restaurant, but it wasn't on my prefix menu.
So I was like, we're going to make it happen.
So I just went over to the venue staff and was like, we got to get this guy the steak that he wants.
Just charge us for whatever it is, but we need to make sure that he gets it.
So I am the one who's making sure that things are moving on time.
I'm the one who's tapping my CEO's shoulder being like, okay, it's time to do your presentation.
It's time to make your comments.
So I'm really, I'm there to watch.
And then once everything's settled, if they want me to stay and sit in there, great.
If they're like, nope, this is closed door.
We just want executives to have conversations that are private.
I'm going to go over to the bar and sit down within ear distance.
So I can kind of hear if there's kind of mic issues going on or anything like that so that I can still close enough to fix anything.
But giving them enough privacy that they can still feel like they can really have some open conversations.
Yeah, awesome.
Have you had to, like, I'm sure you have.
So what is there an event where you or like a series of events where you've designed it, the whole concepts from scratch, like it didn't exist.
The reason I'm asking is because like, we're this company is like, basically, everyone's only worked here for six months.
So like, we need to do everything from scratch.
So I'm curious, like, if you've had to do that, and like, had success there.
Yeah, two times.
I'll give you both of those examples.
So once at Pavilion, that was the roadshow.
My lessons learned is that you don't do nine events over nine weeks across the country.
You don't do that.
Oh, yeah, no.
Sounds terrible.
Yeah, we did it.
And we survived.
Why?
Were you trying to like, condense pipeline generation or something?
Yeah, we wanted to, we wanted to.
We also just wanted to have a lot of the same speakers over and over again, so we didn't want to, like, lose the opportunity of having them, so we kind of just, like, piled it all in.
Yeah.
No, it was 12 weeks, but still, nine events over 12 weeks is rough.
So I worked with the marketing team, and we concept out, like, where we wanted these events to go, and that was first looking at what were the communities that had the biggest executive practice.
And then making sure that we are then targeting those areas to have these events because they're the ones that need it.
And then we worked together with the content team to ensure, like, what we wanted to talk about and sourced the speakers.
I did all the sourcing for the venues.
I did all the negotiations of the venues and the contracts and vendors, all of that fun stuff.
And then, so everything basically from cradle to grave.
On those ones, when it came to planning logistics, I managed.
I even helped support the team with the emails.
It's not, I can do it, and I do it well and do it fine.
It's not my favorite thing to do.
I root for logistics, but I am absolutely capable and successful at writing the emails for these events and helping with kind of drive the people to the events.
And then I also partnered very closely with the head of community here at Drone Deploy to launch what we're calling our Deploy Days.
So these are similar to a roadshow, but it's definitely spread out.
We do one a month, and again, we're looking at where we have the most active products or projects, and then those are the places that we're going to go and host the events.
We're also taking that list of the cities that have the most active projects, and then we're putting it in front of the sales team and saying, okay, which of these do you guys want to host events in?
Because we need your buy-in just as much as we have buy-in on these events.
It's because they're your people and we're making the environment for your opportunities.
So then we worked together to identify where to do them.

And then I go out and I start finding the cool venues and doing all of the planning and helping with managing the registrations, working with the creative team to build out the websites, helping them design, like figuring out what to actually have on the websites, even as far as if I have to do the research to find a registration platform, doing all that or working with the team to build up something like we use Marketo right now, Marketo Forms.
So working with the team to build out all of that.
These events are fun because we don't want it to just be like another lunch and learn.
So I pick places that are fun.
So we have one on Monday that's a, it's at a brewery.
So we're doing networking on the front end.
And then they, we do about a 45 minute presentation.
We always have a customer come and speak because we don't.
We want them just hearing from us.
We want them to hear from people who they care about, projects that they care about.
And then they're doing a brewery tour and tasting right after.
So I've done brewery tour and tastings.
I've done, I mean, obviously Topgolf because everybody does Topgolf.
I've done go-kart racing, which was way more successful than I thought it was going to be.
Those guys really loved getting in those go-karts.
What is the person who goes to the event?
Is it like a head of construction or like?
Yeah, it could be anything from a superintendent or a chief technology officer all the way down to like the pilots that are running.
So we're targeting all of them for these events.
And it's both prospects and customers.
Our goal is 70% customers and then 30% prospects for those events.
Very different from the marketer event types though, I would assume.
I'm like go-karting versus like Michelin dinner.
Yes, it's been interesting to kind of go through these type of events when I've done very different events in my past.
How do you deal with, like, different stakeholders jumping in at random times?
Like, Roussel loves events, too, and, like, has a lot of opinions and thoughts and ideas.
But you know how CEOs are.
They can, like, they're really in it one minute, and then they're just, they've got a million things going on.
like, I don't know, have you had to manage different stakeholders like that?
And do you have a good system to, like, get what you need from them, but also, like, you know, push back on some things, too?
Yeah, so I did work very closely with Sam Jacobs at Pavilion, and he's the CEO.
So, and he is, he's got ideas.
He's got ideas and maybe a smaller group than ideas.
So, so really what it comes down to.
For me, the executive brain wants to see figures and they want to see what's the bottom line.
What's the impact?
So if they're asking for something that's kind of crazy, I'm still going to go do the research and present to them, like, this is what it's going to cost.
This is what we will either gain or lose out by doing it.
It's your choice.
But let me educate you on what that means, what the implications or positive outcomes will be.
So it's really just hearing what they are thinking and then providing the right kind of guidance to either reel it in or push it forward.
Yeah, because sometimes at the end of the day, it's like, all right, we're just going to go for it.
Like, there's that balance.
I mean, this is not even related to events, just in general, working in small companies where the CEO is in the weeds of, like, picking between, like,
Like, they're just wanting to move so fast.
That's usually the underlying thing.
It's just like, move faster.
Let's get this done.
So, you know, when to just go with the flow with what they're suggesting versus, like, really push back and try something different.
And, like, it's totally situational.
It is.
Yeah.
It is.
But I find as long as you're coming in with facts and solutions, that seems to me.
And you just have to, like, disagree and commit all the time.
I'm like, okay, I'd do it differently, but let's try it.
And honestly, I always find, like, usually it's actually, they have great ideas.
There's a reason they're, like, CEOs, right?
It's like, okay, that was way cooler than I thought.
Exactly.
Yes.
Diversity of opinion.
I'll take it.
Exactly.
Yeah.
Okay.
So speaking of, like, measuring, like, numbers or ROI, or, like, what are some of the things you look at when an event's done, if, like, if it was successful or not?
And I'm less about I'm less worried
Are about like specific metrics or something?
I'm just like, what are ways you can look back and be like, I think that was awesome and we shouldn't do it again or not?
Yeah.
So first and foremost, it's, it's always going to be.
Sorry.
I don't know why they're connected right now.
It's always the line pipeline and close one opportunities that, that is, you cannot get away from them.
You have to be able to prove the ROI on the event.
So that's, that's one thing that you were, I'm always looking at.
I'm also looking at attendance to see actually how many people registered versus how many people actually showed up.
That's usually the satisfaction score.
So CSAT and looking at what, what people's had feelings about the event, if they were good or bad.
How do you do that?
Like from a, like surveys or?
Survey.
You can do it from a survey.
can do it from live polls during the, during the event, which is usually your best bet is to do live polls.
Cause you're.
I'm also measuring the impressions that we see on social media.
So if we're seeing people getting out there and posting and saying great things about the event, that's just to me.
And then it depends on, well, and then staying on budget.
For me, that's very important is to stay on budget for the event.
So those would be very budget fluid here.
Okay, well, that's good.
But I, it's not my favorite thing.
It's just that we're, we don't have, we're so early, don't have the infrastructure to measure even like, like, literally, we still don't measure like conversion and pipeline and whatever.
We don't have that all set up yet.
So it's like a little bit going on vibes.
So there's no even, I can't really push back on budget too much either.
It's like...
We do try to split the cost with people though.
Like we love co-hosting stuff.
So that helps for sure.
But, you know, I think like over time, we'll start figuring out how to measure it better.
But for now, yeah, it's funny.
It's a little bit like, but like we, like, they're always asking me like, how much should we spend?
I'm like, I don't know.
There's, you tell me.
Oh, no.
That gives me, that makes me like uncomfortable.
I absolutely will be line item everything that comes.
And like my budget for Horizons, which is our big user conference.
think it's like 80 lines long of like everything that I know I'm going to spend on these things.
Cost it out right down to, I know for the break, I'm probably going to need to have seven gallons of coffee.
So I'm going to cost it what that's going to be like.
I break it down to that level.
Yeah.
Awesome.
Um, what, what's, um, so you mentioned like, you want to get back to working with marketers or anything, what else are you looking for in a next role?
I
Yes, definitely trying to get back into the go-to-market world with all of my people again.
I am looking for a role that allows me the opportunity to kind of build, build something.
I have, I found myself in that role many times over my career where I'm like the first one in.
And I, I like that.
I like innovating.
I like creating.
I love building a process like toolkit.
Toolkits.
That is my jam.
I love building a good toolkit.
So, and doing out a policies and procedures document.
Those, those things I, I actually really enjoy doing, which is weird, but, you know.
Me I'm weird too.
I like thoughts.
So that's, that's the, that's what I'm looking for.
And I took the time to kind of look back through the, the job description last night and it was just like, check.
Yep.
That's what I want.
That's what

I want all those things, somebody who can roll up their sleeves and put out name badges, but also can kind of concept out new ideas and think about strategy and think about ROI and metrics and what have you.
I just want to do more of that.
I want to do a lot of events because that's what I do.
I'm always juggling events at once.
And I want my world to be events and I want to be known as the subject matter expert in that.
And that's, I think, where this role comes in because I really do think that I can do that when it's a completely new role and I can kind of come in and build and help guide and lead.
Yeah, that's exactly what, I mean, we're just so early.
I mean, even the work or maybe have our first renewals like coming up in a few months.
So everything's very early, like creating playbooks.

One reason I like it, this job a lot and working here is like, I like the blank slate, basically everything is a blank slate and you can approach it from like first principles of how it should operate versus like, I actually, one thing I'm having to do actively is like unlearn my SaaS playbooks a little bit because it's a very different like buying journey when it's really, really high touch, really, really quick deal cycles, but like high ACVs.
It's all of these CMO people who know Marcel.
So it's just like completely different from like the traditional, like get this really long, like nine month deal cycle and the AE is working it and like doing all these demos and whatever.
It's just so different.
So for me, I'm trying to like, every time we go to design something new, I'm like, wait, let's just take it from our business model, which is like, everything should be AI native.
And like, everything is about like, how can you make this efficient and fast and repeatable and then start.
So that's one thing I've liked.
It's like a new challenge for sure.
And then it's a very interesting role because I've never worked on a team where there's so many marketers in the leadership.
Like Marcel's a marketer, obviously.
And then Jason is a growth.
He's more like growth hacky.
He's very into the go-to-market engineering stuff and all that.
Kyle, who runs a lot of our account stuff, post-sale.
And he was helping with sales for a bit.
He is like was a VP of growth as well.
So he's owned a lot of pipe gen stuff.
So like there's three already who are like marketers like in the room who have like done events.
So there's definitely like lots of ideas.
There's no shortage of ideas floating around.
But like there's just no time.
Like the getting it right, doing it well, like making it repeatable.
Just details that make it like, like you said, it's like people.
People need it to be special, and, like, you have to put that special touch on things to make it worth people's time.

And, like, speaking of metrics of success, we don't really have ways to track a lot of it, but one thing Marcel thinks about a lot is, like, who's posting, like, who posts that they went to his event and stuff.
Like, it's kind of like a word of mouth, like, oh, I'm in the crew situation.
Like, he does, yeah, there's, we hosted a CMO breakfast last week in our office.
So we have a big office, by the way, that's, like, basically an event space.
There's a few desks, but it's, like, we can just use it for events.
And we hosted a CMO breakfast, which we've done three of in, like, the whole, this year.
So, I don't know, every quarter.
Super chill, like, order breakfast.
We had a barista who was, like, cringy.
Printing pictures on the coffee, and everybody was printing Marcel's face on the lattes, taking photos.
It was funny.
So people posted on LinkedIn, like, I love that.
It was good.
Yeah, so anyways, so there's, like, we want to do, I definitely want you to move, if you're still interested, I definitely want to move you forward to the next round.

And, like, what, the next thing, the next thing we have, we'll have you do is just, like, an exercise to, like, look at one or two of the events we want to do and think about how you would sort of, like, go about executing it, basically.
So, like, and this will all be sent to you, but, like, some of events we host are, like, we do want to do executive dinners and, like, get a good routine of doing them.
And those could be wherever, like, SF is easy because it's just where Marcel is, but, like, I know he wants to do more.
And then these CMO breakfasts, which are super, they're so simple.
Honestly, like, it's just, like, come hang out in the office, basically, kind of situation.
But then we might do some meetups.
We want to be at event, like, conference events, at least.
I don't know about sponsoring, but, like, at least be there and have a happy hour or dinner on the side, like, be where people are.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Interview with Gloria Willis for Events Marketing Manager role at GrowthX](https://fathom.video/share/KMkcnAs1Mh2E5xzKGhXxj1qcqRJLg7yo?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Gloria has extensive experience in high-touch event planning for executives, including at Pavilion and Drone Deploy](https://fathom.video/share/KMkcnAs1Mh2E5xzKGhXxj1qcqRJLg7yo?tab=summary&timestamp=397.0)
  - [GrowthX is seeking someone to fully own event execution, with a focus on creating premium experiences for high-level clients](https://fathom.video/share/KMkcnAs1Mh2E5xzKGhXxj1qcqRJLg7yo?tab=summary&timestamp=2198.0)
  - [The role involves building out processes/playbooks from scratch, as the company is early-stage and growing rapidly](https://fathom.video/share/KMkcnAs1Mh2E5xzKGhXxj1qcqRJLg7yo?tab=summary&timestamp=1221.0)
  - [Cultural fit around proactivity, speaking up, and leveraging AI/automation will be important](https://fathom.video/share/KMkcnAs1Mh2E5xzKGhXxj1qcqRJLg7yo?tab=summary&timestamp=2739.0)

## Topics

### Gloria's Relevant Experience

  - [Led events at Pavilion, including global salon dinners, summits, and a 700-person go-to-market conference](https://fathom.video/share/KMkcnAs1Mh2E5xzKGhXxj1qcqRJLg7yo?tab=summary&timestamp=451.0)
  - [Currently at Drone Deploy managing high-touch proprietary programs](https://fathom.video/share/KMkcnAs1Mh2E5xzKGhXxj1qcqRJLg7yo?tab=summary&timestamp=930.0)
  - [Expertise in executive-level events, from intimate dinners to large conferences](https://fathom.video/share/KMkcnAs1Mh2E5xzKGhXxj1qcqRJLg7yo?tab=summary&timestamp=700.0)
  - [Strong focus on ROI, metrics, and proving event value](https://fathom.video/share/KMkcnAs1Mh2E5xzKGhXxj1qcqRJLg7yo?tab=summary&timestamp=1705.0)

### GrowthX's Event Needs

  - [Regular executive dinners and CMO breakfasts](https://fathom.video/share/KMkcnAs1Mh2E5xzKGhXxj1qcqRJLg7yo?tab=summary&timestamp=2198.0)
  - [Presence at industry conferences (e.g. happy hours, meetups)](https://fathom.video/share/KMkcnAs1Mh2E5xzKGhXxj1qcqRJLg7yo?tab=summary&timestamp=2229.0)
  - [Potential small user conference (\~100 people) next year](https://fathom.video/share/KMkcnAs1Mh2E5xzKGhXxj1qcqRJLg7yo?tab=summary&timestamp=2240.0)
  - [Building out AI-led growth community with webinars/content](https://fathom.video/share/KMkcnAs1Mh2E5xzKGhXxj1qcqRJLg7yo?tab=summary&timestamp=2250.0)

### Role Expectations

  - [Take full ownership of event execution, freeing up Marcel's (CEO) time](https://fathom.video/share/KMkcnAs1Mh2E5xzKGhXxj1qcqRJLg7yo?tab=summary&timestamp=2521.0)
  - [Create premium, high-end experiences aligned with GrowthX's service offering](https://fathom.video/share/KMkcnAs1Mh2E5xzKGhXxj1qcqRJLg7yo?tab=summary&timestamp=2603.0)
  - [Build processes/playbooks from scratch as an early employee](https://fathom.video/share/KMkcnAs1Mh2E5xzKGhXxj1qcqRJLg7yo?tab=summary&timestamp=1221.0)
  - [Leverage network and industry connections](https://fathom.video/share/KMkcnAs1Mh2E5xzKGhXxj1qcqRJLg7yo?tab=summary&timestamp=2662.0)
  - [Eventually take on more strategic planning](https://fathom.video/share/KMkcnAs1Mh2E5xzKGhXxj1qcqRJLg7yo?tab=summary&timestamp=2681.0)

### Company Culture

  - [Fast-paced startup environment with opportunity to build and create](https://fathom.video/share/KMkcnAs1Mh2E5xzKGhXxj1qcqRJLg7yo?tab=summary&timestamp=2278.0)
  - [Values proactivity, speaking up about challenges, and leverage-building mindset](https://fathom.video/share/KMkcnAs1Mh2E5xzKGhXxj1qcqRJLg7yo?tab=summary&timestamp=2739.0)
  - [Uses AI and virtual assistants extensively to drive efficiency](https://fathom.video/share/KMkcnAs1Mh2E5xzKGhXxj1qcqRJLg7yo?tab=summary&timestamp=2349.0)

## Next Steps

  - Gloria to complete exercise planning 1-2 potential GrowthX events
  - Interview to be scheduled with Jason and Marcel next week
  - Gloria to review AI-led growth community site and past webinar content

action_items.description: Send Gloria details for next interview round - exercise to plan 1-2 events
Send Gloria link to AI-led growth community website
Watch previous GrowthX webinar on YouTube for context
Coordinate w/ Tucker to schedule next interview w/ Jason & Marcel for Gloria
